text:
BILATERAL DIAGNOSTIC MAMMOGRAPHY AND TARGETED BREAST AND AXILLARY ULTRASOUND WITH ULTRASOUND-GUIDED FINE NEEDLE ASPIRATION, LEFT AXILLA

EXAMINATION TYPE: Bilateral diagnostic mammography (standard CC and MLO projections, with additional spot compression and magnification views of the left upper outer quadrant) and targeted ultrasound of the left breast and left axilla. Ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration of left axillary lymph node performed at same setting.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 39-year-old premenopausal female, Nigerian-American, presenting with a self-palpated left breast mass at the 10 o'clock position, present for approximately six weeks. Significant family history: mother with breast cancer diagnosed at age 42, maternal aunt with ovarian cancer. No prior personal history of breast disease. No prior mammography on file for comparison.

TECHNIQUE: Standard bilateral two-view digital mammography was obtained. Tomosynthesis (3D digital breast tomosynthesis) obtained in both MLO and CC projections bilaterally. Additional spot-compression magnification views of the left upper outer quadrant were obtained for further characterization. Breast density assessment: Scattered areas of fibroglandular density (ACR category B). Targeted real-time B-mode ultrasound of the left breast was performed with a 12-5 MHz linear array transducer, with systematic survey of the left breast followed by focused evaluation of the palpable area of concern at the 10 o'clock position. Color Doppler interrogation was applied. Evaluation of bilateral axillae was performed, with detailed assessment of the left axilla. Ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration of the left axillary lymph node was performed with a 22-gauge needle under continuous real-time sonographic guidance; three passes were made and material submitted for cytopathology.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
FINDINGS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

MAMMOGRAPHY:

Left Breast:
Within the upper outer quadrant at the 10 o'clock position, 4 cm from the nipple, there is a 3.1 cm irregular high-density mass with markedly spiculated margins. The mass demonstrates a dense central nidus with radiating spicules extending into the surrounding parenchyma. The spicules are long, irregular, and asymmetrically distributed, creating a stellate architectural pattern. There is associated focal architectural distortion of the adjacent parenchyma. No microcalcifications are present within or around the mass. No skin thickening or nipple retraction is evident on mammographic views. No satellite lesions are identified in the left breast. No suspicious axillary adenopathy is seen on the MLO projection, though a prominent left axillary lymph node with apparent cortical thickening is noted (better assessed on ultrasound). Spot-compression magnification views confirm the irregular spiculated morphology of the mass and exclude microcalcification.

Right Breast:
No mass, asymmetry, architectural distortion, or suspicious microcalcification is identified throughout the right breast parenchyma. Right axilla: lymph nodes appear within normal limits with preserved fatty hila on the MLO view.

Bilateral Skin and Nipples:
No skin thickening, trabecular coarsening, or nipple retraction identified bilaterally on mammography.

ULTRASOUND — LEFT BREAST:

At the 10 o'clock position, 4 cm from the nipple, corresponding precisely to the palpable area of concern and the mammographic mass, there is a 3.2 x 2.8 x 2.1 cm (transverse x anteroposterior x craniocaudal) irregular, predominantly solid hypoechoic mass. The mass has angular, microlobulated, and partially spiculated margins. Posterior acoustic shadowing is present, moderate in degree, consistent with the fibrotic/desmoplastic stromal reaction characteristic of invasive carcinoma. The mass is taller than wide in one plane, and oriented non-parallel to the skin surface in the anteroposterior projection, a feature strongly associated with malignancy. The echogenicity is markedly hypoechoic relative to the subcutaneous fat. Internal echotexture is heterogeneous with areas of focal hypoechogenicity suggesting necrosis or intratumoral heterogeneity. No internal calcifications are resolved sonographically. Color Doppler imaging demonstrates markedly increased internal vascularity with multiple penetrating vessels in a central and peripheral distribution, consistent with neovascularity. No skin thickening or Cooper's ligament disruption is identified directly overlying the mass. No satellite nodules or additional suspicious foci are identified on systematic survey of the remainder of the left breast. No ductal ectasia or abnormal intraductal process.

Right Breast Ultrasound:
Systematic survey of the right breast was not the primary focus of this targeted examination; however, no dominant mass or obvious abnormality is appreciated in the portions of the right breast surveyed.

ULTRASOUND — BILATERAL AXILLAE:

Left Axilla:
Within the left axilla at the lower axillary level (level I), there is a single dominant lymph node measuring 2.1 cm in maximum dimension (long axis). This node demonstrates markedly abnormal morphologic features: cortical thickening measuring up to 4.2 mm (normal <3 mm), the thickening being eccentric and lobulated along the cortex. The fatty hilum is absent (complete loss of hilar echogenicity). The lymph node is rounded in overall shape with a long-to-short axis ratio approaching 1.4 (normal >2). Color Doppler demonstrates peripheral hilar vascularity with displacement of the normal hilar flow pattern. The sonographic features of this lymph node are highly suspicious for metastatic involvement. No additional morphologically suspicious lymph nodes are identified in the left axillary chain at this examination, though evaluation of level II and III nodes is limited by depth.

Right Axilla:
Several lymph nodes are visualized in the right axilla, all demonstrating preserved thin cortices (cortical thickness <2 mm) and preserved echogenic fatty hila. No morphologically suspicious lymph nodes in the right axilla.

ULTRASOUND-GUIDED FINE NEEDLE ASPIRATION, LEFT AXILLARY LYMPH NODE:
Under continuous real-time sonographic guidance, the dominant left axillary lymph node was targeted for fine needle aspiration using a 22-gauge needle. Three separate passes were performed under direct visualization. Adequate material was obtained and submitted on glass slides for immediate cytopathologic assessment (if available) and for cell block preparation. The needle was confirmed within the cortex of the target lymph node during aspiration. No immediate procedural complications. Post-procedure sonographic evaluation confirms no hematoma at the FNA site. The aspirated node demonstrated no significant change in appearance immediately post-procedure.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
IMPRESSION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. LEFT BREAST, 10 O'CLOCK POSITION: A 3.1 cm (mammography) / 3.2 cm (ultrasound) irregular mass with spiculated and angular margins, posterior acoustic shadowing, and markedly increased internal vascularity. The morphologic features of this mass on both mammography and ultrasound are highly suspicious for primary malignancy, most likely invasive carcinoma. The mass corresponds precisely to the patient's palpable area of concern.

2. LEFT AXILLARY LYMPHADENOPATHY: A single dominant left axillary lymph node (level I) measuring 2.1 cm with abnormal cortical thickening (up to 4.2 mm), complete loss of the fatty hilum, rounded morphology, and abnormal Doppler flow is identified. The sonographic appearance is highly suspicious for metastatic nodal involvement. Ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration of this node was performed as above; cytopathology results pending.

3. RIGHT BREAST AND AXILLA: No mammographic or sonographic abnormality identified in the right breast. Right axillary lymph nodes appear morphologically normal.

4. No additional satellite lesions, microcalcifications, or evidence of multifocal or multicentric disease identified in the left breast.

5. No imaging findings to suggest skin involvement or nipple-areolar complex involvement at this time.

ACR BI-RADS CATEGORY: 5 — Highly Suggestive of Malignancy (left breast mass and left axillary lymph node). Tissue sampling is indicated; core needle biopsy of the left breast mass is recommended for histopathologic diagnosis if not already obtained. FNA of the left axillary lymph node has been performed at this setting.

Performing Radiologist: [Radiologist name on file]
Interpreting Radiologist: [Interpreting radiologist name on file]
Dictated and finalized: [Date to be assigned programmatically]
